title: The boilerplate problem
instruction:
Every class you have written so far has a lot of repetitive setup:
- `__init__` to store fields
- `__repr__` for debugging
- `__eq__` for comparison

For a class with 5 fields, that is 20+ lines just for setup. `@dataclass` generates all of it.

Run this. Compare: same result, half the code.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

# Without @dataclass — 12 lines of boilerplate:
class ProductManual:
    def __init__(self, name, price, in_stock):
        self.name = name
        self.price = price
        self.in_stock = in_stock
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Product(name={self.name!r}, price={self.price!r}, in_stock={self.in_stock!r})"

# With @dataclass — 4 lines:
@dataclass
class Product:
    name: str
    price: float
    in_stock: bool

p = Product("Laptop", 999.99, True)
print(p)
print(p.name)
successMessage: @dataclass generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ automatically from the field declarations. Same result, dramatically less code.

title: Create a dataclass
instruction:
A dataclass uses type annotations to declare fields:

```python
@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float
```

Create a `User` dataclass with `name`, `email`, and `age` fields. Create two instances and print them.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

# TODO: define User dataclass with name, email, age
# Create two instances and print them

codeChecks.message: Create at least one User instance.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three fields, no __init__ written, no __repr__ written. @dataclass handled it. The type annotations (: str, : int) are declarations — Python uses them to build __init__.

title: Default values
instruction:
Fields can have default values:

```python
@dataclass
class Config:
    host: str = "localhost"
    port: int = 8080
    debug: bool = False
```

Fields with defaults must come after fields without.

Add defaults to `is_active` (True) and `role` ("user") in the dataclass.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class User:
    name: str
    email: str
    # TODO: add is_active with default True and role with default "user"

user1 = User("Alice", "alice@example.com")
user2 = User("Bob", "bob@example.com", is_active=False, role="admin")
print(user1)
print(user2)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: user1 uses all defaults, user2 overrides two. Dataclass defaults work just like function defaults. Fields with defaults must always come last.

title: __eq__ is automatic
instruction:
Dataclasses automatically generate `__eq__` — two instances with the same field values are equal.

Run this to confirm.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float

p1 = Point(3.0, 4.0)
p2 = Point(3.0, 4.0)
p3 = Point(1.0, 2.0)

print(p1 == p2)  # True — same values
print(p1 == p3)  # False — different values
print(p1 is p2)  # False — different objects
successMessage: Same values = equal, even though they are different objects. @dataclass generates __eq__ automatically. With plain classes you would have written this yourself.

title: frozen=True — immutable dataclasses
instruction:
`@dataclass(frozen=True)` makes the dataclass immutable — like a tuple, but with named fields.

Trying to modify a frozen dataclass raises `FrozenInstanceError`.

Run this. Frozen is perfect for constants, config, and anything that should not change.
successMessage: Frozen dataclasses are immutable. They can also be used as dictionary keys (unlike regular dataclasses). Perfect for value objects like colours, coordinates, config.

title: __post_init__ — validate after init
instruction:
`__post_init__` runs automatically after `__init__`. Use it for validation:

```python
def __post_init__(self):
    if self.age < 0:
        raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")
```

Add `__post_init__` to validate that price is positive and stock is non-negative.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: __post_init__ gives you the best of both worlds: @dataclass generates the boilerplate, and you add custom validation. Clean and powerful.

title: Build an order system
instruction:
No starter code.

Create two dataclasses:
- `Item(name, price, quantity)` — frozen, with a `total` property (price × quantity)
- `Order(items, customer_name)` — mutable, with a `total()` method that sums all item totals

Create an order with 3 items and print the total.
successMessage: Immutable items, mutable order. A clean separation of concerns using dataclasses. Chapter 17 done.
